title: High-fidelity fast fluorescence lifetime imaging by event-based denoising - Nature Biotechnology
text:
AbstractFluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) enables quantitative measurement of molecular environments, interactions and protein conformations. However, a large number of photons are required for accurate lifetime determination, restricting its practical applications in fast deep-tissue imaging. Here, we present event-based first-photon FLIM (EFLIM), a self-supervised denoising method that infers fluorescence lifetime at extremely low light. By representing each excitation event as a binary process instead of histogram accumulation, EFLIM reduces photon requirement by over two orders of magnitude compared to state-of-the-art algorithms, leading to an apparent mean lifetime measurement below one photon per pixel with strong robustness to intensity artifacts. To demonstrate EFLIM’s applicability, we observed transient intracellular dynamics of ligand-dependent molecular states, captured putative vesicle-mediated contacts between different lymphocytes through multiplexed imaging in a single spectral channel and achieved rapid label-free visualization of tumor heterogeneity in human glioma tissue. These results illustrate EFLIM’s strong potential in neuroscience, cell biology, immunology and pathology by probing dynamic molecular processes in vivo.

Data availability

All data supporting the findings of this study are provided in the paper and the Supplementary Information. A representative raw dataset (SPC file) corresponding to Fig. 4, together with the associated SPC-to-TIFF reconstruction scripts, is publicly available from GitHub (https://github.com/BBNCELi/EFLIM). Additional raw data are available from the corresponding authors upon reasonable request because of the large size of the raw time-resolved imaging datasets. Source data are provided with this paper.
Code availability

The MATLAB code for photon arrival simulations and the Python implementation of EFLIM are available from GitHub (https://github.com/BBNCELi/EFLIM).

Calcium activities in cortical neurons of awake mice (depth: ~250 μm). EFLIM rejects motion-induced and intensity-induced fluctuations, yielding stable lifetime estimates compared to CMM and FPFLI.Supplementary Video 2 (download MP4 )EFLIM enables quantitative measurement of intracellular calcium signaling. Live HeLa cells loaded with OGB-1 AM were imaged at ~0.2–0.5 PPP. Under these photon-limited conditions, CMM and FPFLI failed to resolve lifetime fluctuations, while EFLIM robustly captured transient lifetime increases (2.5–3.3 ns).Supplementary Video 3 (download MP4 )EFLIM enables multiplexed imaging of lymphocytes with a single spectral channel. Lifetime-resolved volumetric imaging of GC B cells (orange) and follicular helper T cells (green) over 2 h at a depth of ~60 μm. EFLIM revealed distinct migration patterns, direct T–B contacts and secretion of vesicles whose fluorescence lifetimes decreased from ~2.3 ns to ~1.6 ns over 25–30 min.Source dataSource Data Fig. 2 (download XLSX )Source data, including lifetime SNR values and phasor plot data.Source Data Fig. 3 (download XLSX )Source data, including PPP and lifetime measurements.Source Data Fig. 4 (download XLSX )Source data, including lifetime measurements from intracellular calcium imaging.Source Data Fig. 5 (download XLSX )Source data, including cell velocity, cell lifetime, instantaneous speed, lifetime decrease, spatial location and track-averaged speed data.Rights and permissionsSpringer Nature or its licensor (e.g. a society or other partner) holds exclusive rights to this article under a publishing agreement with the author(s) or other rightsholder(s); author self-archiving of the accepted manuscript version of this article is solely governed by the terms of such publishing agreement and applicable law.Reprints and permissionsAbout this articleCite this articleZhou, Y., Xiao, Y., Zhou, J. et al.
